behaviors.description: The cron parser rejects invalid expressions with descriptive error messages
behaviors.justification: Cron parsing is a pure, deterministic function: string input → validation → error or success. No I/O, subprocess, or external state involved. A mock cannot hide parsing bugs because there's nothing to mock — the parser either validates correctly or fails. Real failures (e.g., accepting month 13, missing validation checks) are caught at the function boundary with simple unit test fixtures.
behaviors.test_description: Verify the parser rejects invalid cron expressions (month 13, day 32, malformed syntax, invalid field ranges) and returns descriptive error messages for each invalid case

behaviors.description: Next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps for standard and edge-case cron expressions
behaviors.justification: Computing next run times is algorithmic calendar logic with no I/O or external dependencies. A mock cannot hide this failure — the function either computes the right timestamp or doesn't. Real bugs (off-by-one errors in month boundaries, incorrect leap-year handling, wrong nearest-weekday calculation) are directly exposed by comparing computed timestamps to expected values. The test can provide controlled inputs (start times, cron expressions) and verify outputs deterministically.
behaviors.test_description: Calculate next run times for standard cron expressions (every 5 AM, daily, weekly) and edge cases (last day of month, nearest weekday, month/year boundaries, leap years); verify each computed timestamp matches the expected run time

behaviors.description: Overlap detector identifies schedules firing within the minimum gap and reports conflicting time windows
behaviors.justification: The overlap detector is a comparison algorithm that takes two sets of run times and identifies conflicts. Bugs (off-by-one gap interpretation, missing edge cases like exactly-at-boundary or multiple overlaps, incorrect conflict window reporting) are caught by testing the detector with fixture-based time lists — no need to integrate with the calculator. Hand-crafted time lists can be constructed to match realistic schedule patterns. Mocking is not a concern because the detector is given data, not dependencies.
behaviors.test_description: Create fixture time lists for two schedules with known overlap patterns (overlapping within gap, exactly at gap boundary, no overlap); verify the detector correctly identifies all conflicts within the configured minimum gap and reports accurate conflicting time windows for each case

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling produces correct run times across DST transitions
behaviors.justification: DST handling cannot be tested with mocks without hiding real failures. DST rules are deterministic but complex (region-specific, year-specific, with ambiguities during fall-back). A mock with hardcoded rules would miss bugs that emerge from real timezone calculations. The test must use a real timezone library (bundled tzdata) integrated with the cron logic to expose bugs like: incorrect gap handling during spring-forward, incorrect ambiguity resolution during fall-back, or off-by-one errors near the transition time. The OS or library's authoritative timezone database is the source of truth.
behaviors.test_description: Set up jobs scheduled for 2:30 AM in a timezone with DST transitions; compute next run times across spring-forward (when 2:00–3:00 AM is skipped) and fall-back (when 2:00–3:00 AM repeats); verify the engine correctly determines which occurrence is meant and computes times that account for the skipped/repeated hour

behaviors.description: Missed-run policy: engine determines whether to skip or backfill based on configured policy when next run time has already passed
behaviors.justification: The missed-run policy is deterministic logic: if next_run_time < current_time, apply the configured action (skip, backfill, or backfill-with-limit). Real bugs (wrong policy branching, incorrect backfill count, missing policy check) are caught by testing the policy logic in isolation with controlled time inputs (mocked/injected current time). No I/O, subprocess, or external state required. The key test is to set up scenarios (e.g., next run 2 hours ago, policy is 'backfill') and verify the engine produces the correct action.
behaviors.test_description: Configure jobs with different missed-run policies (skip, backfill, backfill-with-limit); simulate past next-run-times by setting current time beyond scheduled runs; verify the engine correctly detects each missed run and applies the configured policy, producing the expected action for each case
